name: engraphis-memory
description: Replace the built-in markdown memory store with Engraphis — persistent, scoped, explainable memory for your coding agent (self-hosted server at ENGRAPHIS_URL). The built-in `memory` tool, auto-reflect, /remember & /forget, compaction extracts, and session priming all route to Engraphis. Ten extra tools expose Engraphis's query & curation surface (grounded recall, why/timeline, pin, correct, consolidate, link, record_event).
system_prompt:
## Memory: Engraphis

Your long-term memory is backed by a self-hosted **Engraphis** server (persistent, scoped, bi-temporal, explainable). It replaces the markdown memory store.

- The built-in **`memory`** tool stores and forgets durable facts via Engraphis. Use it (action `save`/`append`/`forget`/`list`) exactly as you would the markdown store — for durable facts, decisions, conventions, user preferences, and bug causes/fixes worth keeping across sessions. NOT for transient state or things already in the codebase.
- **Session priming** is automatic: at session start, proactive recall injects "what matters right now" across all workspaces.
- Memories are scoped: `workspace` scope → per-codebase (Engraphis workspace + repo basename); `global` scope → cross-codebase facts.
- Conflicts resolve deterministically: an exact restatement reinforces; a same-subject update **supersedes** (closes, never deletes) the old one.

### Extra Engraphis tools (when you need more than store/forget)

- **engraphis_recall** — hybrid semantic + lexical search returning a LIST of matches with scores. Use before answering questions about past decisions/conventions.
- **engraphis_recall_grounded** — a direct, cited ANSWER from memory, or an explicit **abstain** if evidence is insufficient. Use when you need an answer, not a list. Do NOT guess when it abstains.
- **engraphis_why** — the current answer AND the facts it superseded (what changed and when). Use to explain why something is the way it is.
- **engraphis_timeline** — bi-temporal history of a topic (oldest first). Use to trace how a fact evolved.
- **engraphis_stats** — memory-bank overview (counts by type/workspace).
- **engraphis_pin** — exempt a memory from decay/pruning (important conventions).
- **engraphis_correct** — supersede a wrong memory with replacement content (keeps the supersession chain). Prefer over forget+remember when you have replacement content.
- **engraphis_consolidate** — sleep-time distillation sweep (episodic→semantic); set dry_run=true to preview.
- **engraphis_link** — connect two related memories a plain recall wouldn't surface as connected.
- **engraphis_record_event** — lightweight episodic log entry (lower ceremony than `memory` for raw events to be consolidated later).

If the Engraphis server is unreachable, tools return a hint instead of crashing the turn; writes surface the error so you know memories aren't persisting.

tools.name: engraphis_recall
tools.description: Hybrid semantic + lexical + retention search over the memory bank. Returns a LIST of matching memories with scores. Use to find relevant past facts before answering or acting.

query.description: What to look for (a question or topic).

repo.description: Repo scope within the workspace. Defaults to the current directory name.

tools.name: engraphis_recall_grounded
tools.description: Get a direct ANSWER from memory, with [n] citations — or an explicit abstain if evidence is insufficient. Offline/deterministic (no LLM synthesis). Use when you need an answer, not a list.

query.description: The question to answer from memory.

repo.description: Repo scope within the workspace. Defaults to the current directory name.

tools.name: engraphis_why
tools.description: The current answer to a question AND the facts it superseded (what used to be true but changed). Use to explain why something is the way it is, including past decisions that were overturned.

question.description: The question to answer from memory.

tools.name: engraphis_timeline
tools.description: Bi-temporal history of a topic: what was believed and when, oldest first. Use to trace how a fact evolved.

tools.name: engraphis_stats
tools.description: Overview of the memory bank: total memories, breakdown by type, workspaces, sessions.

workspace.description: Scope to a workspace, or omit for all.

tools.name: engraphis_pin
tools.description: Pin a memory so it is exempt from automatic decay and pruning. Use for important conventions that must persist.

tools.name: engraphis_correct
tools.description: Supersede a memory with new content — the old version is closed (kept in history, never deleted) and the new content becomes current truth. Prefer this over forget+remember when you have replacement content, because it preserves the supersession chain (visible via engraphis_why / engraphis_timeline).

tools.name: engraphis_consolidate
tools.description: Run a sleep-time consolidation sweep: recurring episodic memories on the same subject are distilled into one durable semantic digest, and fully-decayed transient memories are archived. Use periodically to keep the memory bank lean. Set dry_run=true to preview without changing anything.

dry_run.description: If true, preview only — nothing is modified. Default false.

tools.name: engraphis_link
tools.description: Explicitly connect two related memories that a plain recall wouldn't surface as connected (e.g. a bug report and the memory describing the fix). Use when you notice two stored facts are related.

tools.name: engraphis_record_event
tools.description: Append a lightweight episodic log entry — lower ceremony than the `memory` tool, for raw events you may later want consolidated into a durable fact (e.g. 'tried X, it deadlocked'). Use the `memory` tool for crisp facts; use this for event logs that accumulate.

repo.description: Repo scope within the workspace. Defaults to the current directory name.
